title: Rhabdomyolysis
oneLiner: A syndrome of muscle necrosis releasing intracellular contents into circulation, often from crush, exertion, or toxins; can cause acute kidney injury (AKI) and life-threatening electrolyte derangements.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
You’re called to the emergency department: A 22-year-old male, brought in after being found lying on the floor for ~12 hours following a heroin overdose. He is confused, complaining of severe bilateral thigh and arm pain, and his urine is a dark cola colour. His blood pressure is 110/70, pulse 105, temperature 37.8°C. On exam, his thighs are swollen, tender, and he cannot lift his legs against gravity. His calves are also tense but with intact distal pulses.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Prolonged immobility + opioid overdose** → prolonged compression of muscles → ischemia and necrosis
- **Dark cola-coloured urine** → myoglobinuria (myoglobin filtered into urine)
- **Severe muscle pain and weakness** → direct muscle damage; unable to lift legs → functionally significant rhabdomyolysis
- **Swollen, tense, tender muscles** → oedema from intracellular contents leaking; risk of compartment syndrome
- **Tachycardia, mild hypotension** → hypovolaemia from fluid sequestration into damaged muscle
- **Low-grade fever** → inflammatory response to muscle necrosis

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Silent rhabdomyolysis:** No pain or weakness — only discovered on routine labs (raised CK) e.g. in statin therapy or post-seizure.
- **Delayed presentation:** Days after a crush injury, patient presents only with oliguria (AKI); no obvious muscle symptoms.
- **Focal rhabdomyolysis:** Only one muscle group affected (e.g. calf after strenuous squatting) — can mimic deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or cellulitis.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Rhabdomyolysis is the breakdown of skeletal muscle fibres that spill their contents — myoglobin, potassium, phosphate, creatine kinase (CK), and urate — into the bloodstream. This toxic soup can damage the kidneys and cause dangerous electrolyte imbalances.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Common — estimated 20,000 cases/year in the US (likely underreported). Any patient with muscle pain + dark urine must be screened.
- **Stakes:** Up to 30% of cases develop AKI; mortality 5-10% if AKI supervenes. Hyperkalaemia can cause arrhythmias; compartment syndrome can lead to limb loss.
- **Common pitfalls:** Under-resuscitating fluids early, ignoring rising CK, and failing to check for concurrent hyperkalaemia or hypocalcaemia.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:** Muscle cells rely on ATP to maintain membrane integrity. When energy supply fails (due to ischaemia, toxins, extreme exertion, or metabolic exhaustion), the Na+/K+ ATPase pump fails. Sodium and calcium flood the cell, leading to swelling, protease activation, and mitochondrial damage. The cell membrane ruptures, spilling contents into the interstitium and bloodstream.

**Why each key sign happens:**
- **Dark urine (myoglobinuria):** Myoglobin (17.8 kDa) is filtered by glomeruli, but in acidotic urine, it dissociates into ferrihemate, which is toxic to proximal tubules. Also casts form.
- **Hyperkalaemia:** Damaged cells release massive intracellular K+. Potassium can rise quickly, especially in crush or exertional cases.
- **Acute kidney injury (AKI):** Three mechanisms — (1) tubular obstruction by myoglobin casts, (2) direct tubular toxicity from free iron, (3) renal vasoconstriction due to volume depletion and endothelin release.
- **Hypocalcemia:** Phosphate released from muscle binds calcium, causing early hypocalcemia. Later, as disease resolves, calcium may rebound (often confused with hypercalcemia).
- **Elevated CK:** Creatine kinase is abundant in muscle. CK levels correlate with muscle mass injured. CK > 5 x ULN (> 1000 U/L) is diagnostic; levels > 5000 U/L strongly associated with AKI.
- **Muscle swelling (in crush):** Intracellular oedema leads to compartment syndrome if fascia is inelastic. Pain disproportionate to exam is a red flag.

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The immobile overdose patient had prolonged pressure → ischaemia → necrosis → release of K+, myoglobin, CK. Dark urine = myoglobinuria. Tachycardia = hypovolaemia from fluid trapped in muscles.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Examples | Stem Clue |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Traumatic/Compressive** | Crush injury, prolonged immobilisation (fall, surgery, drugs/alcohol), compartment syndrome, electrical burns | "Found on floor for hours", "Car accident with trapped limb", "Seizure >30min" |
| **Exertional** | Extreme exercise (especially in untrained individuals), heatstroke, sickle cell trait | "Completed a marathon in hot weather", "Hit the gym after a month off" |
| **Toxic** | Alcohol, cocaine, amphetamines, MDMA, LSD, statins (especially simvastatin with CYP3A4 inhibitors), colchicine, fibrates, lithium, antipsychotics (stiff-man syndrome), snake/insect venoms | "Uses cocaine", "On simvastatin + clarithromycin", "Hx of a recent party with ecstasy" |
| **Metabolic/Endocrine** | Hypothyroidism, diabetic ketoacidosis, electrolyte disturbances (hypokalaemia, hypophosphataemia), hyperosmolar states | "Known hypothyroidism, new muscle weakness", "DKA patient with muscle aches" |
| **Infectious** | Influenza, COVID-19, coxsackie (Bornholm disease in children), bacterial myositis (Streptococcus, Staphylococcus), Legionella, sepsis | "Fever, myalgias, recent viral illness", "COVID-19 positive with CK > 2000" |
| **Autoimmune** | Polymyositis, dermatomyositis, vasculitis (polyarteritis nodosa) | "Proximal muscle weakness over weeks, heliotrope rash" |
| **Genetic** | McArdle disease (glycogen storage type V), carnitine palmitoyltransferase deficiency, Duchenne muscular dystrophy, malignant hyperthermia (RYR1 mutation) | "Recurrent rhabdo with exercise", "Family history of anaesthesia reactions" |

**Most common causes globally:** Traumatic (including crush and immobilisation) and exertional. In hospitalised patients, drugs and infections are frequent triggers.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (consensus):** Acute onset muscle symptoms + CK ≥ 5 times upper limit of normal (> 1000 U/L) — no single scoring system used universally.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Serum CK (total)** | CK > 1000 U/L confirms rhabdomyolysis | Essential for diagnosis; trend tracks disease progression | CK 5–10x ULN mild; > 5000 high risk of AKI; > 50,000 massive injury |
| **Urine dipstick (blood +)** | Positive for blood, no red cells on microscopy | Screens for myoglobinuria | Dipstick positive + no RBCs = myoglobinuria. Confirms rhabdomyolysis if CK elevated. |
| **Serum K+** | Hyperkalaemia | Rule out life-threatening arrhythmia | K+ > 6.0 → immediate ECG monitor + treatment. Also low K can be cause (hypokalaemic rhabdo). |
| **Serum Ca2+** | Hypocalcaemia (early) | Monitor for complications; dictates fluid choice | Low Ca + high phosphate = early rhabdo. Late: may rise (rebound). |
| **Serum phosphate** | Hyperphosphataemia | Phosphate release from muscle | High phosphate worsens hypocalcaemia. |
| **Acid-base (VBG/ABG)** | Metabolic acidosis (lactic acidosis + hyperkalaemia) | Assess severity of tissue hypoperfusion | Base excess > -6 may indicate crush syndrome. |
| **Urine myoglobin** | Confirm myoglobinuria | Background: more sensitive than dipstick but slower | Rarely needed clinically; CK and dipstick suffice. |
| **Renal function (creatinine, BUN)** | Rising creatinine → AKI | Assess kidney involvement | Creatinine rise indicates acute tubular necrosis (ATN). |
| **ECG** | Peaked T waves, wide QRS | Screen for hyperkalaemia | K+ > 5.5 → T peaked; > 6.5 → QRS widening; > 7.0 → sine wave / arrest. |

**Pattern recognition:** A **single finding that confirms rhabdomyolysis**: CK > 1000 U/L with a positive dipstick for blood and no RBCs on microscopy. **Single finding that rules out:** CK < 200 U/L with no myoglobinuria.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Aggressive IV fluids:** 0.9% normal saline (NS) – start at 500-1000 mL/hour (or 15-20 mL/kg bolus if hypotensive). Aim for urine output > 200-300 mL/hour. Why: volume expansion dilutes myoglobin and prevents cast formation; corrects hypovolaemia.
- **Treat hyperkalaemia immediately** if K+ > 6.0 or ECG changes:
  - Calcium gluconate 10%: 10 mL IV over 2-5 minutes (stabilises myocardium) — do not give with bicarbonate in same line.
  - Insulin 10 units + dextrose 50% (50 mL) IV bolus — shifts K+ intracellularly.
  - Salbutamol 5 mg nebulised — alternative shift.
  - Consider Ca2+ polystyrene sulfonate (resin) — but slow.
- **Urinary alkalinisation** (controversial but often used in severe crush): Add 100 mmol NaHCO3 (~ 2 amps 8.4%) to 1 L 0.45% NaCl, run at 150-200 mL/hour, target urine pH ≥ 6.5. Why: alkaline urine reduces myoglobin cast formation. Risk: worsens hypocalcaemia if precipitates.
- **Mannitol** (not routinely recommended unless compartment syndrome present) — 1 g/kg IV. May cause volume overload.
- **Paralyse and ventilate if malignant hyperthermia** is suspected (dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus, repeat to 10 mg/kg max).

**Ward (24-72h):**
- **Continue IV fluids** (NS or balanced crystalloid like Ringer's lactate) at 200-300 mL/hour, guided by urine output. Reassess CK and creatinine every 6-12 hours. Stop when CK < 5000 and falling.
- **Monitor urine output** hourly – target > 1 mL/kg/hour. If output falls < 0.5 mL/kg/h despite fluids, consider furosemide 40-80 mg IV (only after volume replete) – but caution: loop diuretics may worsen cast formation if combined with hypovolaemia.
- **Electrolyte monitoring:** K+, Ca2+, phosphate, Mg2+ every 6-8 hours. Correct hyperkalaemia as above. Hypocalcaemia: treat only if symptomatic (tetany, seizures) or if hyperkalaemia requires calcium. Asymptomatic hypocalcaemia: leave alone — calcium is sequestered and will rebound.
- **Compartment syndrome vigilance:** Check compartment pressures if pain out of proportion, pallor, paraesthesia, paralysis. Cutoff: > 30 mmHg (or within 20 mmHg of diastolic BP) → urgent fasciotomy.
- **Haemodialysis indication:** Severe hyperkalaemia refractory to medical therapy, metabolic acidosis, volume overload, or rising creatinine (> 3-4 mg/dL or > 350 µmol/L).

**Long-term:**
- **Discontinue offending drugs** (statins, NSAIDs, nephrotoxic agents, lithium).
- **Avoid NSAIDs** — they worsen renal hypoperfusion.
- **Stretching and physical therapy** after CK < 1000 and muscle pain resolved.
- **Genetic counselling** if recurrent episodes, especially in young males with family history.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- **Do not use sodium bicarbonate routinely** (only in severe crush with acidosis and after ensuring adequate volume and calcium). It causes hypocalcaemia and left shift of oxygen dissociation curve.
- **Do not give furosemide as first-line** – it can drop BP and worsen AKI if patient is hypovolaemic.
- **Do not give calcium for asymptomatic hypocalcaemia** – it may precipitate tissue calcium-phosphate deposition.
- **Do not forget to stop NSAIDs** – they are often given for muscle pain but are nephrotoxic.
- **Do not forget to check compartment pressures** if symptoms are unilateral or disproportionately painful.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Rhabdomyolysis | Viral Myositis | Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) | Compartment Syndrome (without rhabdo) |
|---------|---------------|----------------|-----------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Pain | Bilateral, symmetrical, often proximal (thighs, trunk) | Diffuse myalgias (typically whole body) | Unilateral calf or thigh; sharp, worse with walking | Unilateral, out of proportion to injury; pain with passive stretch |
| Exam | Tender, swollen muscle groups; weakness | Tenderness ± swollen joints; weakness rare | Call tenderness, oedema, Homan's sign (unreliable) | Tense compartment, pain on passive stretch, paraesthesia, pulseless (late) |
| Urine | Dark cola (myoglobinuria); dip + blood, no RBCs | Usually clear; rarely dark but no myoglobin | Clear; no hematuria | Clear; may be dark if concurrent rhabdo |
| Labs | CK > 1000, high K, low Ca, high Phos | CK < 500; normal K/Ca/Phos; viral serology | Normal CK; D-dimer elevated; US shows thrombus | CK may be normal initially; compartment pressure > 30 mmHg |
| Key diff | Dipstick blood + no RBCs + CK > 1000 = rhabdo | Viral prodrome, multiple systemic symptoms; CK rarely > 1000 | Venous duplex ultrasound positive; no muscle enzyme elevation | Invasive pressure measurement; fasciotomy needed; CK rises if rhabdo develops |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Acute Kidney Injury (AKI):** Most feared. Presents with oliguria, rising creatinine, hyperkalaemia, metabolic acidosis. If missed, may progress to anuria and require dialysis. **Key sign:** urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 hours. **What to do:** aggressive fluids, alkalinisation (if severe), early nephrology referral.

**Hyperkalaemia:** Can cause cardiac arrest. **Late presentation:** patient collapsed with wide QRS on monitor. **What to do:** immediate calcium + insulin/dextrose; consider dialysis.

**Compartment Syndrome:** In crush injuries, muscle oedema within tight fascia causes ischaemia → further necrosis → irreversible nerve and muscle damage. **Key signs:** pain with passive stretch, paraesthesia (early), pulselessness (late). **What to do:** measure compartment pressure; if > 30 mmHg, perform fasciotomy.

**Late complications:** 
- **Hypercalcemia (rebound):** 4-7 days later, as calcium re-enters circulation from healing muscle. May cause confusion, constipation, polyuria. Treat with IV fluids; rarely needs bisphosphonates.
- **Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) – rare.** Muscle thromboplastin released can trigger intravascular clotting. Presents with bleeding, bruising, low platelets, elevated D-dimer.
- **Recurrent rhabdomyolysis:** Consider metabolic myopathy (McArdle, CPT deficiency). Muscle biopsy ± genetic testing.

**Late presentation patterns stem test:** "A patient presents with anuria, confusion, and palpable muscle swelling 4 days after a crush injury. What complication?" Answer: AKI due to rhabdomyolysis. "What is the most immediate danger?" Hyperkalaemia.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
- **"RHABDO" – Risk factors:**
  R – Restraint / immobilisation (drugs, alcohol, compressive)
  H – Hyperthermia (heatstroke, malignant hyperthermia)
  A – Alcohol / amphetamines / cocaine
  B – Bodybuilding (extreme exertion)
  D – Drugs (statins, colchicine, antipsychotics)
  O – Other (infections, metabolic, genetics)

- **"MUD PILES" – Causes of lactic acidosis (common in rhabdo):**
  M – Metformin / methanol
  U – Uremia
  D – DKA / drugs
  P – Propylene glycol / paracetamol
  I – Ischemia (shock, crush)
  L – Leukaemia / lymphoma
  E – Ethanol
  S – Sepsis / seizures

- **"C-C-A" for AKI prevention in rhabdo:**
  C – Crystalloid (NS)
  C – Correct K+ and Ca2+
  A – Alkalinise (if severe / acidotic)

**High-yield clinical pearls:**
1. **CK half-life ~2-4 days** – peak at 24-36 hours. If CK rises after 48 hours, suspect ongoing muscle injury (e.g. compartment syndrome or new crush).
2. **Myoglobin is cleared quickly** (half-life 2-4 hours) – a negative urine dipstick does not rule out rhabdo if patient is >12 hours post injury and well-hydrated. CK is more sensitive.
3. **Dark urine does not mean myoglobin alone** – also consider haemoglobinuria (MAHA, transfusion reaction) and porphyria. Dipstick differentiates: myoglobinuria = blood+ but no RBCs.
4. **Hypocalcemia in early rhabdomyolysis** – if Ca < 2.0 mmol/L, avoid giving IV calcium unless symptomatic. It will precipitate in renal tubules. Instead, treat hyperkalaemia and wait.
5. **Statins + CYP3A4 inhibitors** (clarithromycin, itraconazole, grapefruit juice) → high risk of myopathy/rhabdo. Use atorvastatin with caution; avoid simvastatin >20 mg with amiodarone.
6. **In exertional rhabdo, use balanced crystalloid** (Ringer’s) to avoid hyperchloremia acidosis from large volume 0.9% NS.
7. **Compartment syndrome in rhabdo:** if CK > 20,000 and increasing, check compartments even without classic signs.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Forgetting to check urine dipstick.
- Giving lactulose for hyperkalaemia (doesn’t work acutely).
- Using bicarbonate in hypocalcaemia.
- Prescribing NSAIDs for pain.
- Delaying fluids waiting for confirmed diagnosis.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior (registrar/consultant):**
- **NEWS2 > 5** (high): especially if NEWS2 triggered by hypotension, tachycardia, or low SpO2.
- **Rising CK > 50,000 U/L** despite fluids → risk of massive injury, AKI, compartment syndrome.
- **Creatinine doubling within 24 hours** or > 500 µmol/L (5.6 mg/dL) → nephrology referral.
- **ECG showing hyperkalaemia (peaked T, wide QRS)** – call senior immediately, then treat.
- **Urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/h for > 6 hours** after adequate fluid resuscitation (2-3 L NS given) → call.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** (if from crush or exertional) with no alternative cause.
- **GCS < 13** (if associated with drug overdose or cerebral oedema from hyponatraemia from fluid therapy).
- **Suspected compartment syndrome** (compartment pressure > 30 mmHg or falling diastolic BP) → inform orthopaedics and ICU.
- **Signs of malignant hyperthermia** (temperature > 40°C, rigidity, acidosis, CK rise) → crash team, dantrolene.

**When to call ICU / HDU:**
- Need for haemodialysis or CRRT due to AKI.
- Continuous monitoring for hyperkalaemia arrhythmias.
- Compartment syndrome requiring fasciotomy.
- Haemodynamic instability (sepsis from myonecrosis, DIC).
- Airway compromise from muscle swelling (e.g. cervical crush).

**Remember:** Rhabdomyolysis is a clinical diagnosis triggered by a simple urine dip. Early aggressive fluids are the single most effective intervention. Do not wait for lab confirmation to start fluids if the story fits.

rawMarkdown:
## Rhabdomyolysis
**One-liner:** A syndrome of muscle necrosis releasing intracellular contents into circulation, often from crush, exertion, or toxins; can cause acute kidney injury (AKI) and life-threatening electrolyte derangements.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
You’re called to the emergency department: A 22-year-old male, brought in after being found lying on the floor for ~12 hours following a heroin overdose. He is confused, complaining of severe bilateral thigh and arm pain, and his urine is a dark cola colour. His blood pressure is 110/70, pulse 105, temperature 37.8°C. On exam, his thighs are swollen, tender, and he cannot lift his legs against gravity. His calves are also tense but with intact distal pulses.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Prolonged immobility + opioid overdose** → prolonged compression of muscles → ischemia and necrosis
- **Dark cola-coloured urine** → myoglobinuria (myoglobin filtered into urine)
- **Severe muscle pain and weakness** → direct muscle damage; unable to lift legs → functionally significant rhabdomyolysis
- **Swollen, tense, tender muscles** → oedema from intracellular contents leaking; risk of compartment syndrome
- **Tachycardia, mild hypotension** → hypovolaemia from fluid sequestration into damaged muscle
- **Low-grade fever** → inflammatory response to muscle necrosis

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Silent rhabdomyolysis:** No pain or weakness — only discovered on routine labs (raised CK) e.g. in statin therapy or post-seizure.
- **Delayed presentation:** Days after a crush injury, patient presents only with oliguria (AKI); no obvious muscle symptoms.
- **Focal rhabdomyolysis:** Only one muscle group affected (e.g. calf after strenuous squatting) — can mimic deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or cellulitis.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
**Plain-language definition:** Rhabdomyolysis is the breakdown of skeletal muscle fibres that spill their contents — myoglobin, potassium, phosphate, creatine kinase (CK), and urate — into the bloodstream. This toxic soup can damage the kidneys and cause dangerous electrolyte imbalances.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Common — estimated 20,000 cases/year in the US (likely underreported). Any patient with muscle pain + dark urine must be screened.
- **Stakes:** Up to 30% of cases develop AKI; mortality 5-10% if AKI supervenes. Hyperkalaemia can cause arrhythmias; compartment syndrome can lead to limb loss.
- **Common pitfalls:** Under-resuscitating fluids early, ignoring rising CK, and failing to check for concurrent hyperkalaemia or hypocalcaemia.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**The story:** Muscle cells rely on ATP to maintain membrane integrity. When energy supply fails (due to ischaemia, toxins, extreme exertion, or metabolic exhaustion), the Na+/K+ ATPase pump fails. Sodium and calcium flood the cell, leading to swelling, protease activation, and mitochondrial damage. The cell membrane ruptures, spilling contents into the interstitium and bloodstream.

**Why each key sign happens:**
- **Dark urine (myoglobinuria):** Myoglobin (17.8 kDa) is filtered by glomeruli, but in acidotic urine, it dissociates into ferrihemate, which is toxic to proximal tubules. Also casts form.
- **Hyperkalaemia:** Damaged cells release massive intracellular K+. Potassium can rise quickly, especially in crush or exertional cases.
- **Acute kidney injury (AKI):** Three mechanisms — (1) tubular obstruction by myoglobin casts, (2) direct tubular toxicity from free iron, (3) renal vasoconstriction due to volume depletion and endothelin release.
- **Hypocalcemia:** Phosphate released from muscle binds calcium, causing early hypocalcemia. Later, as disease resolves, calcium may rebound (often confused with hypercalcemia).
- **Elevated CK:** Creatine kinase is abundant in muscle. CK levels correlate with muscle mass injured. CK > 5 x ULN (> 1000 U/L) is diagnostic; levels > 5000 U/L strongly associated with AKI.
- **Muscle swelling (in crush):** Intracellular oedema leads to compartment syndrome if fascia is inelastic. Pain disproportionate to exam is a red flag.

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The immobile overdose patient had prolonged pressure → ischaemia → necrosis → release of K+, myoglobin, CK. Dark urine = myoglobinuria. Tachycardia = hypovolaemia from fluid trapped in muscles.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
| Category | Examples | Stem Clue |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Traumatic/Compressive** | Crush injury, prolonged immobilisation (fall, surgery, drugs/alcohol), compartment syndrome, electrical burns | "Found on floor for hours", "Car accident with trapped limb", "Seizure >30min" |
| **Exertional** | Extreme exercise (especially in untrained individuals), heatstroke, sickle cell trait | "Completed a marathon in hot weather", "Hit the gym after a month off" |
| **Toxic** | Alcohol, cocaine, amphetamines, MDMA, LSD, statins (especially simvastatin with CYP3A4 inhibitors), colchicine, fibrates, lithium, antipsychotics (stiff-man syndrome), snake/insect venoms | "Uses cocaine", "On simvastatin + clarithromycin", "Hx of a recent party with ecstasy" |
| **Metabolic/Endocrine** | Hypothyroidism, diabetic ketoacidosis, electrolyte disturbances (hypokalaemia, hypophosphataemia), hyperosmolar states | "Known hypothyroidism, new muscle weakness", "DKA patient with muscle aches" |
| **Infectious** | Influenza, COVID-19, coxsackie (Bornholm disease in children), bacterial myositis (Streptococcus, Staphylococcus), Legionella, sepsis | "Fever, myalgias, recent viral illness", "COVID-19 positive with CK > 2000" |
| **Autoimmune** | Polymyositis, dermatomyositis, vasculitis (polyarteritis nodosa) | "Proximal muscle weakness over weeks, heliotrope rash" |
| **Genetic** | McArdle disease (glycogen storage type V), carnitine palmitoyltransferase deficiency, Duchenne muscular dystrophy, malignant hyperthermia (RYR1 mutation) | "Recurrent rhabdo with exercise", "Family history of anaesthesia reactions" |

**Most common causes globally:** Traumatic (including crush and immobilisation) and exertional. In hospitalised patients, drugs and infections are frequent triggers.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria (consensus):** Acute onset muscle symptoms + CK ≥ 5 times upper limit of normal (> 1000 U/L) — no single scoring system used universally.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Serum CK (total)** | CK > 1000 U/L confirms rhabdomyolysis | Essential for diagnosis; trend tracks disease progression | CK 5–10x ULN mild; > 5000 high risk of AKI; > 50,000 massive injury |
| **Urine dipstick (blood +)** | Positive for blood, no red cells on microscopy | Screens for myoglobinuria | Dipstick positive + no RBCs = myoglobinuria. Confirms rhabdomyolysis if CK elevated. |
| **Serum K+** | Hyperkalaemia | Rule out life-threatening arrhythmia | K+ > 6.0 → immediate ECG monitor + treatment. Also low K can be cause (hypokalaemic rhabdo). |
| **Serum Ca2+** | Hypocalcaemia (early) | Monitor for complications; dictates fluid choice | Low Ca + high phosphate = early rhabdo. Late: may rise (rebound). |
| **Serum phosphate** | Hyperphosphataemia | Phosphate release from muscle | High phosphate worsens hypocalcaemia. |
| **Acid-base (VBG/ABG)** | Metabolic acidosis (lactic acidosis + hyperkalaemia) | Assess severity of tissue hypoperfusion | Base excess > -6 may indicate crush syndrome. |
| **Urine myoglobin** | Confirm myoglobinuria | Background: more sensitive than dipstick but slower | Rarely needed clinically; CK and dipstick suffice. |
| **Renal function (creatinine, BUN)** | Rising creatinine → AKI | Assess kidney involvement | Creatinine rise indicates acute tubular necrosis (ATN). |
| **ECG** | Peaked T waves, wide QRS | Screen for hyperkalaemia | K+ > 5.5 → T peaked; > 6.5 → QRS widening; > 7.0 → sine wave / arrest. |

**Pattern recognition:** A **single finding that confirms rhabdomyolysis**: CK > 1000 U/L with a positive dipstick for blood and no RBCs on microscopy. **Single finding that rules out:** CK < 200 U/L with no myoglobinuria.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Aggressive IV fluids:** 0.9% normal saline (NS) – start at 500-1000 mL/hour (or 15-20 mL/kg bolus if hypotensive). Aim for urine output > 200-300 mL/hour. Why: volume expansion dilutes myoglobin and prevents cast formation; corrects hypovolaemia.
- **Treat hyperkalaemia immediately** if K+ > 6.0 or ECG changes:
  - Calcium gluconate 10%: 10 mL IV over 2-5 minutes (stabilises myocardium) — do not give with bicarbonate in same line.
  - Insulin 10 units + dextrose 50% (50 mL) IV bolus — shifts K+ intracellularly.
  - Salbutamol 5 mg nebulised — alternative shift.
  - Consider Ca2+ polystyrene sulfonate (resin) — but slow.
- **Urinary alkalinisation** (controversial but often used in severe crush): Add 100 mmol NaHCO3 (~ 2 amps 8.4%) to 1 L 0.45% NaCl, run at 150-200 mL/hour, target urine pH ≥ 6.5. Why: alkaline urine reduces myoglobin cast formation. Risk: worsens hypocalcaemia if precipitates.
- **Mannitol** (not routinely recommended unless compartment syndrome present) — 1 g/kg IV. May cause volume overload.
- **Paralyse and ventilate if malignant hyperthermia** is suspected (dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus, repeat to 10 mg/kg max).

**Ward (24-72h):**
- **Continue IV fluids** (NS or balanced crystalloid like Ringer's lactate) at 200-300 mL/hour, guided by urine output. Reassess CK and creatinine every 6-12 hours. Stop when CK < 5000 and falling.
- **Monitor urine output** hourly – target > 1 mL/kg/hour. If output falls < 0.5 mL/kg/h despite fluids, consider furosemide 40-80 mg IV (only after volume replete) – but caution: loop diuretics may worsen cast formation if combined with hypovolaemia.
- **Electrolyte monitoring:** K+, Ca2+, phosphate, Mg2+ every 6-8 hours. Correct hyperkalaemia as above. Hypocalcaemia: treat only if symptomatic (tetany, seizures) or if hyperkalaemia requires calcium. Asymptomatic hypocalcaemia: leave alone — calcium is sequestered and will rebound.
- **Compartment syndrome vigilance:** Check compartment pressures if pain out of proportion, pallor, paraesthesia, paralysis. Cutoff: > 30 mmHg (or within 20 mmHg of diastolic BP) → urgent fasciotomy.
- **Haemodialysis indication:** Severe hyperkalaemia refractory to medical therapy, metabolic acidosis, volume overload, or rising creatinine (> 3-4 mg/dL or > 350 µmol/L).

**Long-term:**
- **Discontinue offending drugs** (statins, NSAIDs, nephrotoxic agents, lithium).
- **Avoid NSAIDs** — they worsen renal hypoperfusion.
- **Stretching and physical therapy** after CK < 1000 and muscle pain resolved.
- **Genetic counselling** if recurrent episodes, especially in young males with family history.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- **Do not use sodium bicarbonate routinely** (only in severe crush with acidosis and after ensuring adequate volume and calcium). It causes hypocalcaemia and left shift of oxygen dissociation curve.
- **Do not give furosemide as first-line** – it can drop BP and worsen AKI if patient is hypovolaemic.
- **Do not give calcium for asymptomatic hypocalcaemia** – it may precipitate tissue calcium-phosphate deposition.
- **Do not forget to stop NSAIDs** – they are often given for muscle pain but are nephrotoxic.
- **Do not forget to check compartment pressures** if symptoms are unilateral or disproportionately painful.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
| Feature | Rhabdomyolysis | Viral Myositis | Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) | Compartment Syndrome (without rhabdo) |
|---------|---------------|----------------|-----------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Pain | Bilateral, symmetrical, often proximal (thighs, trunk) | Diffuse myalgias (typically whole body) | Unilateral calf or thigh; sharp, worse with walking | Unilateral, out of proportion to injury; pain with passive stretch |
| Exam | Tender, swollen muscle groups; weakness | Tenderness ± swollen joints; weakness rare | Call tenderness, oedema, Homan's sign (unreliable) | Tense compartment, pain on passive stretch, paraesthesia, pulseless (late) |
| Urine | Dark cola (myoglobinuria); dip + blood, no RBCs | Usually clear; rarely dark but no myoglobin | Clear; no hematuria | Clear; may be dark if concurrent rhabdo |
| Labs | CK > 1000, high K, low Ca, high Phos | CK < 500; normal K/Ca/Phos; viral serology | Normal CK; D-dimer elevated; US shows thrombus | CK may be normal initially; compartment pressure > 30 mmHg |
| Key diff | Dipstick blood + no RBCs + CK > 1000 = rhabdo | Viral prodrome, multiple systemic symptoms; CK rarely > 1000 | Venous duplex ultrasound positive; no muscle enzyme elevation | Invasive pressure measurement; fasciotomy needed; CK rises if rhabdo develops |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
**Acute Kidney Injury (AKI):** Most feared. Presents with oliguria, rising creatinine, hyperkalaemia, metabolic acidosis. If missed, may progress to anuria and require dialysis. **Key sign:** urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 hours. **What to do:** aggressive fluids, alkalinisation (if severe), early nephrology referral.

**Hyperkalaemia:** Can cause cardiac arrest. **Late presentation:** patient collapsed with wide QRS on monitor. **What to do:** immediate calcium + insulin/dextrose; consider dialysis.

**Compartment Syndrome:** In crush injuries, muscle oedema within tight fascia causes ischaemia → further necrosis → irreversible nerve and muscle damage. **Key signs:** pain with passive stretch, paraesthesia (early), pulselessness (late). **What to do:** measure compartment pressure; if > 30 mmHg, perform fasciotomy.

**Late complications:** 
- **Hypercalcemia (rebound):** 4-7 days later, as calcium re-enters circulation from healing muscle. May cause confusion, constipation, polyuria. Treat with IV fluids; rarely needs bisphosphonates.
- **Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) – rare.** Muscle thromboplastin released can trigger intravascular clotting. Presents with bleeding, bruising, low platelets, elevated D-dimer.
- **Recurrent rhabdomyolysis:** Consider metabolic myopathy (McArdle, CPT deficiency). Muscle biopsy ± genetic testing.

**Late presentation patterns stem test:** "A patient presents with anuria, confusion, and palpable muscle swelling 4 days after a crush injury. What complication?" Answer: AKI due to rhabdomyolysis. "What is the most immediate danger?" Hyperkalaemia.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics:**
- **"RHABDO" – Risk factors:**
  R – Restraint / immobilisation (drugs, alcohol, compressive)
  H – Hyperthermia (heatstroke, malignant hyperthermia)
  A – Alcohol / amphetamines / cocaine
  B – Bodybuilding (extreme exertion)
  D – Drugs (statins, colchicine, antipsychotics)
  O – Other (infections, metabolic, genetics)

- **"MUD PILES" – Causes of lactic acidosis (common in rhabdo):**
  M – Metformin / methanol
  U – Uremia
  D – DKA / drugs
  P – Propylene glycol / paracetamol
  I – Ischemia (shock, crush)
  L – Leukaemia / lymphoma
  E – Ethanol
  S – Sepsis / seizures

- **"C-C-A" for AKI prevention in rhabdo:**
  C – Crystalloid (NS)
  C – Correct K+ and Ca2+
  A – Alkalinise (if severe / acidotic)

**High-yield clinical pearls:**
1. **CK half-life ~2-4 days** – peak at 24-36 hours. If CK rises after 48 hours, suspect ongoing muscle injury (e.g. compartment syndrome or new crush).
2. **Myoglobin is cleared quickly** (half-life 2-4 hours) – a negative urine dipstick does not rule out rhabdo if patient is >12 hours post injury and well-hydrated. CK is more sensitive.
3. **Dark urine does not mean myoglobin alone** – also consider haemoglobinuria (MAHA, transfusion reaction) and porphyria. Dipstick differentiates: myoglobinuria = blood+ but no RBCs.
4. **Hypocalcemia in early rhabdomyolysis** – if Ca < 2.0 mmol/L, avoid giving IV calcium unless symptomatic. It will precipitate in renal tubules. Instead, treat hyperkalaemia and wait.
5. **Statins + CYP3A4 inhibitors** (clarithromycin, itraconazole, grapefruit juice) → high risk of myopathy/rhabdo. Use atorvastatin with caution; avoid simvastatin >20 mg with amiodarone.
6. **In exertional rhabdo, use balanced crystalloid** (Ringer’s) to avoid hyperchloremia acidosis from large volume 0.9% NS.
7. **Compartment syndrome in rhabdo:** if CK > 20,000 and increasing, check compartments even without classic signs.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Forgetting to check urine dipstick.
- Giving lactulose for hyperkalaemia (doesn’t work acutely).
- Using bicarbonate in hypocalcaemia.
- Prescribing NSAIDs for pain.
- Delaying fluids waiting for confirmed diagnosis.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call your senior (registrar/consultant):**
- **NEWS2 > 5** (high): especially if NEWS2 triggered by hypotension, tachycardia, or low SpO2.
- **Rising CK > 50,000 U/L** despite fluids → risk of massive injury, AKI, compartment syndrome.
- **Creatinine doubling within 24 hours** or > 500 µmol/L (5.6 mg/dL) → nephrology referral.
- **ECG showing hyperkalaemia (peaked T, wide QRS)** – call senior immediately, then treat.
- **Urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/h for > 6 hours** after adequate fluid resuscitation (2-3 L NS given) → call.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** (if from crush or exertional) with no alternative cause.
- **GCS < 13** (if associated with drug overdose or cerebral oedema from hyponatraemia from fluid therapy).
- **Suspected compartment syndrome** (compartment pressure > 30 mmHg or falling diastolic BP) → inform orthopaedics and ICU.
- **Signs of malignant hyperthermia** (temperature > 40°C, rigidity, acidosis, CK rise) → crash team, dantrolene.

**When to call ICU / HDU:**
- Need for haemodialysis or CRRT due to AKI.
- Continuous monitoring for hyperkalaemia arrhythmias.
- Compartment syndrome requiring fasciotomy.
- Haemodynamic instability (sepsis from myonecrosis, DIC).
- Airway compromise from muscle swelling (e.g. cervical crush).

**Remember:** Rhabdomyolysis is a clinical diagnosis triggered by a simple urine dip. Early aggressive fluids are the single most effective intervention. Do not wait for lab confirmation to start fluids if the story fits.
